title: Persistence Implant After Breach (Linux)
briefing: After a valid-credential login for $USER1 on $TARGET from $SRC1, three things happened on the host: a new local account was created, an SSH key was written to an authorized_keys file, and a cron job was installed. The attacker is nailing down persistent access. Identify every persistence mechanism so none are missed during eviction.
dashboard_hint: agent.name: $TARGET. rule.id 5715 (login) then 5902 (new user), 100210 (authorized_keys implant), 100215 (cron). Read full_log on the 1002xx alerts. Each is an INDEPENDENT foothold — miss one and the attacker walks back in after you reset the password.

questions.prompt: What is the attacker doing on $TARGET?
questions.explain: New account + backdoor SSH key + cron job are three independent persistence mechanisms.
All of the activity lands on $TARGET; the other hosts are unaffected.

questions.prompt: Which persistence mechanisms were used? (select all)
questions.explain: 5902 new user, 100210 authorized_keys implant, 100215 cron. The Windows options do not apply to a Linux host.

questions.prompt: You reset $USER1's password. Is the attacker locked out?
options: No — the backdoor key, rogue account and cron job all still grant access
Yes — a password reset fully evicts them
No — but only because of the cron job
questions.answer: No — the backdoor key, rogue account and cron job all still grant access
questions.explain: Each foothold is independent of $USER1's password. Full eviction means removing the rogue user, the implanted key, AND the cron job.

questions.prompt: Which account did the attacker log in as to plant persistence?
questions.explain: The 5715 login and subsequent commands ran as $USER1.
Account creation, authorized_keys, and cron — three persistence sub-techniques. No impact/encryption occurred.

options: Rotate $USER1 and audit all keys/accounts on the host
answer: Rotate $USER1 and audit all keys/accounts on the host
questions.explain: Pull every foothold and audit the host; a password reset alone leaves three ways back in.
